introduction.p:
Consider a spherical tank of radius 4 m that is filling with water. Let V be the volume of water in the tank (in cubic meters) at a given time, and h the depth of the water (in meters) at the same time. It can be shown using calculus that V is a function of h according to the rule
V = f(h) = \frac{\pi}{3} h^2(12-h) .

statement.p: What values of h make sense to consider in the context of this function? What values of V make sense in the same context?

solution.p: Since depth can't be negative and can't exceed the tank's diameter of 8 m, the values of h that make sense are 0 \le h \le 8. The corresponding values of V range from 0 (empty tank) to f(8) = \frac{256\pi}{3} (full tank), so 0 \le V \le \frac{256\pi}{3}.

statement.p: What is the domain of the function f in the context of the spherical tank? Why? What is the corresponding codomain? Why?

solution.p: The domain of f in context is [0, 8], since the water depth can range from 0 m (empty) to 8 m (full diameter). The range is \left[0, \frac{256\pi}{3}\right]. Since volumes are non-negative, the codomain can be taken as [0, \infty), or more broadly as all real numbers if we consider the abstract formula without physical constraints.

statement.p: Determine and interpret (with appropriate units) the values f(2), f(4), and f(8). What is important about the value of f(8)?

solution.p:
f(2) &= \frac{\pi}{3}(4)(10) = \frac{40\pi}{3} \approx 41.9 \text{ m}^3 f(4) &= \frac{\pi}{3}(16)(8) = \frac{128\pi}{3} \approx 134.0 \text{ m}^3 f(8) &= \frac{\pi}{3}(64)(4) = \frac{256\pi}{3} \approx 268.1 \text{ m}^3
These give the volume of water when the depth is 2, 4, and 8 meters respectively. The value f(8) = \frac{256\pi}{3} equals the volume of a sphere of radius 4, confirming that the tank is completely full when h = 8.

statement.p: Consider the claim: since f(9) = \frac{\pi}{3} 9^2(12-9) = 81\pi \approx 254.47, when the water is 9 meters deep, there is about 254.47 cubic meters of water in the tank. Is this claim valid? Why or why not? Further, does it make sense to observe that f(13) = -\frac{169\pi}{3}? Why or why not?

solution.p: Neither claim is valid. The domain of f is [0, 8], so h = 9 is outside the domain — the water cannot be 9 m deep in a tank whose diameter is only 8 m. Similarly, f(13) falls outside the domain, and the negative value it produces is further evidence that the formula should not be applied beyond h = 8.

statement.p: Can you determine a value of h for which f(h) = 300 cubic meters? Why or why not?

solution.p: No. The maximum value of f on its domain [0, 8] is f(8) = \frac{256\pi}{3} \approx 268.1 m^3, which is less than 300. The tank simply cannot hold 300 cubic meters of water.
